title: Visitor Log
desc: Even failed diplomacy still runs on stamps, signatures, and making sure nobody enters the wrong room.

steps.text:
The embassy's visitor table stands just inside the front hall beneath a carved lintel that says, in polished OverWorld script, WELCOME, and in practical Danian reality, state your business before touching anything. Most days the log records couriers, maintenance workers, and the occasional Danian functionary who wishes to speak to someone who writes slower and argues less loudly than the hive's own clerks.

Today the log keeper has a queue. A Danian courier has arrived with sealed correspondence and impatience. Behind him stands a supply carrier holding a crate of inkstone and looking increasingly certain he is in the wrong building. The log keeper slides the tablet toward you. "Take one. Verify the courier's credentials, or walk the carrier through the entry protocol before he decides paperwork is an OverWorld plot."

options.label: Check the courier's seal and enter the correspondence properly
Help the confused carrier through visitor protocol

steps.text:
The courier produces three things at once: an attitude, a seal tube, and a dispatch strip marked with hive authorization. You check the strip against the day's accepted marks, confirm the wax impression is intact, and record the sender, destination, and transfer time in the embassy ledger while he watches with theatrical impatience.

"You know what this is," he says. "It arrived from the central chambers."

"That is why I am logging it instead of guessing," you reply.

He considers objecting further, then notices the log keeper pretending not to listen and decides against it. Once the final line is entered, the correspondence is stamped, routed, and handed off to the inner office without any room for dispute later. The courier leaves with the offended dignity of someone denied the pleasure of being dramatically delayed.

The log keeper reviews your entry, then nods. "Good. The point of a visitor log is not trust. It is memory. Trust changes its mind; ink stays where you put it."

options.label: Blot the fresh entry and call the next visitor forward

steps.text:
The carrier's mistake is simple enough. He can read Danian direction markers, but the embassy uses OverWorld room labels, entry notation, and a signing order that makes perfect sense to the staff and nobody else. You walk him through each step: declare cargo, state destination, note whether the crate will remain on site, then sign the lower line, not the upper one.

By the second explanation he looks less suspicious and more embarrassed. "In the hive," he says, pressing his seal ring into the clay pad, "if you are carrying supplies, everyone assumes you are meant to keep carrying them until someone stops you."

"Here," says the log keeper without looking up, "we prefer to know which room gets to regret the delivery."

That earns the carrier's first laugh. Once the form is complete, you escort him to the receiving room and make sure the inkstone crate lands where it was actually ordered. On the way back, the log keeper has already marked the next line ready for use. "Excellent," she says. "We have transformed confusion into documentation. That is the embassy at its best."

options.label: Return to the visitor table before the line grows again
